intro:
Zerubbabel (Hebrew Zerubbavel, likely "offspring of Babylon," i.e. born in exile; called Zorobabel in the Greek of the New Testament) was the Davidic prince who led the first return of Jewish exiles from Babylon and governed Judah during the rebuilding of the temple. A grandson of the captive king Jehoiachin, he stood in the royal line of David (1 Chronicles 3:17–19), and so his leadership kept alive the hope of the Davidic monarchy even under Persian rule, where he served as governor (pechah) of the restored province.

Under the decree of Cyrus around 538 BC, Zerubbabel led a great company of returning exiles back to Jerusalem (Ezra 2:1–2). Together with the high priest Jeshua (Joshua) son of Jehozadak, he first rebuilt the altar and restored the sacrifices and the feasts, then laid the foundation of the second temple amid mingled shouts of joy and the weeping of old men who remembered Solomon's house (Ezra 3:2–3, 10–13). When the surrounding peoples offered to help, Zerubbabel refused to compromise the work, and their resulting opposition stalled the building for some fifteen years (Ezra 4:1–5).

The deadlock broke in 520 BC, when the prophets Haggai and Zechariah stirred Zerubbabel and Jeshua to resume (Ezra 5:1–2; Haggai 1:14), and the temple was completed in 516 BC (Ezra 6:15). The two prophets invested Zerubbabel with striking significance. Haggai delivered the oracle, "I will take you, O Zerubbabel... and make you like a signet ring, for I have chosen you" (Haggai 2:23) — a deliberate reversal of the judgment on his grandfather, whom God had sworn to tear off "though you were the signet ring on my right hand" (Jeremiah 22:24). Zechariah's vision of the golden lampstand answered the smallness of the work with the great word, "Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD", promising that the hands that laid the foundation would also bring forth the capstone (Zechariah 4:6–10).

Zerubbabel's enduring importance lies in this restoration of the worshipping community and the keeping of the Davidic promise through the exile. The second temple he built became the house into which, centuries later, the Messiah himself would come — "the latter glory of this house shall be greater than the former" (Haggai 2:9). Both Gospel genealogies trace the line of Jesus through him, as Zorobabel, linking the returned governor to great David's greater Son (Matthew 1:12–13; Luke 3:27). In him the chosen line, nearly extinguished in Babylon, was preserved and carried forward, and the work of God advanced not by human strength but by his Spirit.
hitchcock_meaning: a stranger at Babylon; dispersion of confusion
